title: Banana production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
India is by far the world’s largest banana producer. In 2023, it produced 36.6 million tonnes - more than a quarter of global output. 
That’s more than double the production of the next highest country, China, and far ahead of major exporters like the Philippines and Ecuador. 
Despite its scale, however, most of India’s bananas are consumed domestically, with less than 1% being exported internationally.

title: Electric car sales share
source: International Energy Agency (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
While most countries are seeing year-on-year growth in electric vehicle (EV) adoption, Germany is bucking the trend. 
In 2022, 31% of new cars sold in Germany were electric, but by 2024, that share had dropped to just 19%. 
A key reason is the sharp reduction in government incentives, including the abrupt end of EV purchase subsidies for private buyers in late 2023. 
In contrast, Norway continues to lead the transition, with a staggering 92% of new car sales in 2024 being electric.

title: Share of women in parliament
infoDescription:
Between 1994 and 2024, Rwanda increased the share of women in Parliament from 4.3% to 63.8%.
It became the first country to reach a female majority in its lower house in 2008 and has maintained it since. 
As of 2024, only six countries have reached or exceeded 50%: Cuba and Nicaragua surpass it, while Mexico, Namibia, and the UAE have achieved exact parity. 
Globally, the average remains just 26.4%.

title: Objects launched into space
infoDescription:
In 2024, the United States launched 2,263 objects into space, maintaining its global leadership in space activities. 
This accounted for roughly 76% of the 2,963 total objects launched worldwide that year. 
It launched just over 100 objects in space in 2016. This shows the massive expansion of private-sector missions, especially mega-constellation deployments such as Starlink.

title: Patent applications
subtitle: Per million people
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2021, South Korea filed around 3,600 patent applications per million people - the highest rate in the world. 
This marks a sharp rise over recent years, rising from only 33 patent applications per million people in 1980. 
Japan, once the global leader, has seen its patent rate decline since 2000. 
The global average is just over 300 per million people.

title: Coffee bean production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2023, Brazil produced approximately 3.41 million tonnes of coffee beans, accounting for about 31% of global coffee production. 
This makes Brazil the world’s largest coffee bean producer by a wide margin. 
Vietnam and Indonesia follow as the second and third largest producers.

title: Deaths in armed conflicts
subtitle: Based on where they occurred
source: Uppsala Conflict Data Program (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
More than 400 thousand combatants and civilians died due to fighting in Syria between 2011 and 2024.
The conflict deaths peaked in 2014 with over 78,000 fatalities.
While the numbers have declined since, Syria still saw thousands of conflict-related deaths each year through 2024. 
Countries like Ukraine and Ethiopia show more recent spikes.

title: Military spending as a share of GDP
infoDescription:
Ukraine’s defence expenditure surged dramatically after Russia’s full‑scale invasion in 2022, rising to a maximum of 36.5% of its GDP in 2023. 
This represents an unprecedented jump from under 4% in pre‑war years and places Ukraine at the top globally in recent years.
In comparison, Russia's military spending represents 7.1% of its GDP in 2024.
The second highest rate in 2024 is far behind at 8.8% in Israel, and 7.3% in Saudi Arabia.

title: Chicken meat production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
China is the world’s third-largest producer of chicken meat, with output reaching about 14.8 million tonnes in 2023. 
This places it just behind Brazil at 14.83 million tonnes and behind the United States at around 20 million tonnes. 
China's chicken production has expanded drastically in the 1990s, making it a dominant global supplier and consistently among the top three producers.

title: Alcohol consumption
subtitle: In liters of pure alcohol per person
infoDescription:
In 2020, Romania recorded the highest alcohol consumption rate in the world, at 16.8 litres of pure alcohol per person aged 15 and over.
It has consistently remained at the top since 2000, ahead of Georgia and Latvia. 
Romania’s per capita consumption is more than three times the global average of about 5 litres of pure alcohol per person.

title: Share of electricity production from nuclear
infoDescription:
Since the mid-1980s, nuclear power has been the largest source of electricity in France.
As of 2024, 68% of the country’s electricity is generated from nuclear energy, compared to a global average of just under 10%.
France ramped up its nuclear program after the 1973 oil crisis and now operates 56 nuclear reactors.

title: Estimated nuclear warhead stockpiles
source: Federation of American Scientists (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
As of 2024, Russia is estimated to possess approximately 4,380 nuclear warheads. 
This is significantly lower than the Cold War peak of over 40,000 warheads in the 1987.
It remains however the largest stockpile globally, ahead of the United States.

title: Fish and seafood consumption
subtitle: In Kg per person
infoDescription:
In 2022, the Maldives recorded a per capita fish and seafood consumption of approximately 80 kg per person per year, ranking second globally, only behind Iceland. 
This figure is about four times the global average of 20.5 kg. Other countries with high fish consumption include South Korea and Portugal.

title: GDP per capita
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2023, Luxembourg topped the list with about 132,000 international dollars GDP per capita, just ahead of Singapore, Qatar, and Ireland. 
This figure is more than six times higher than the global average of 20,600$ per capita. 
Luxembourg’s exceptionally high ranking reflects its sizable non-resident workforce and strong financial-services sector, despite a modest dip in 2023.

title: Tree cover loss from wildfires
subtitle: In million hectares
source: Global Forest Watch (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2023, Canada experienced approximately 7.79 million hectares of tree cover loss due to wildfires, the highest in the world ahead of Russia and Australia. 
This represents a significant spike compared to previous years and highlights Canada’s outsized contribution to global fire-related forest loss. 
Rising temperatures and changing climate conditions are widely linked to the surge in wildfire activity driving these losses.

title: Academic papers published
subtitle: Per million people
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Denmark ranks second globally in scientific publications per capita. In 2020, it published 2,627 academic papers per million people.
It is just behind Switzerland, which has held the top spot consistently since 1996.
The global average in 2020 was 373 publications per million people.

title: Share of the population with access to electricity
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Between 2010 and 2023, South Sudan had the lowest share of population with electricity access in the world. 
In 2023, only about 5.4% of South Sudan’s population had access to even basic electricity - up from just around 0.8% in 2007.
As of 2023, only two other countries had less than 15% electricity access: Burundi and Chad.

title: Deaths from drug use
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, the United States had the highest death rate from drug use disorders, with approximately 32 deaths per 100,000 people. 
This was notably higher than Canada, which had the second-highest rate at 19 per 100,000. 
By comparison, the global average stood at just 2 deaths per 100,000 people.

title: Hospital beds
subtitle: Per 1,000 people
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2020, Japan had 12.72 hospital beds per 1,000 people, the second highest rate in the world. 
This is nearly four times the global average of 3.28 beds per 1,000 people. 
Over the past four decades, Japan has maintained a high hospital bed capacity, while many other countries have seen declines. 
South Korea was just behind in 2020 with 12.65 beds per 1,000 people.

title: Deaths from homicide
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
source: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
El Salvador’s homicide rate fell sharply over recent years, reaching just 7.9 per 100,000 people in 2022. 
This represents a striking decline of more than tenfold since 2015, when the rate was approximately 107 per 100,000. 
The drop is widely attributed to a combination of aggressive security policies, expanded policing and surveillance, and a crackdown on gangs.

title: Wine production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2022, Italy was the world’s largest wine producer, with production reaching approximately 5.4 million tonnes. 
This placed it ahead of France, which produced about 4.6 million tonnes, and Spain, with around 3.6 million tonnes. 
The United States followed in fourth place, producing roughly 2.4 million tonnes.

title: Per capita greenhouse gas emissions
subtitle: In tonnes of CO₂ equivalent
source: Jones et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Qatar’s per‑person greenhouse gas emissions reached 68.5 tonnes of CO₂ equivalent in 2023, down from 129 tonnes per person in 1997, but consistently the highest in the wrold since 1992.
This is over ten times the global average of about 6.7 tonnes of CO₂ equivalent per person. 
Bahrain is the second‑highest, emitting around 42.7 tonnes of CO₂ equivalent per person.

title: Life expectancy
subtitle: In years
infoDescription:
Lesotho’s life expectancy at birth stood at only 51.5 years as of 2021, the lowest in the world. 
This is well below the global average of about 71 years. 
Only three other countries in the world have life expectancy at birth of less than 55 years: the Central African Republic, Somalia and Eswatini.

title: Proportion of labor force who are women
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Afghanistan’s share of women in the labor force dropped to approximately 6.8% in 2024, the lowest rate in the world for that year. 
This is sharply below the global average of about 40%. 
The share has declined markedly from its peak of around 22% in 2017, highlighting a steep fall in women's participation over recent years. 
This collapse corresponds to the return of the Taliban in 2021 and subsequent restrictions that have effectively barred most Afghan women from formal employment.

title: Population
infoDescription:
India’s population has continued to rise steadily and in 2023 it surpassed China to become the most populous country in the world. 
According to UN projections, India’s population is expected to keep growing through the mid-21st century, reaching around 1.6 to 1.7 billion before beginning a gradual decline. 
Unlike China, India’s growth has not yet peaked, largely due to its younger age structure and higher fertility rate, though this rate has been falling over time. 
By the end of the century, India is still projected to remain the world’s largest population center.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Venezuela holds the world’s largest proven oil reserves and depends heavily on oil revenues.
In the early 2000s, it was producing around 2.9 million barrels per day, making it one of the top oil producers in the world.
Since 2015, production has fallen sharply, dropping to under 1 million barrels per day by 2024.
Over this period, the United States imposed sanctions, including financial restrictions and oil trade limits that made it harder for Venezuela to sell and finance its crude.

title: Number of cattle
infoDescription:
In 2023, Brazil had the largest cattle herd in the world, with over 230 million head of cattle. 
India has the second largest herd with about 195 million head, and the United States ranks third with around 95 million cattle in 2023.

title: Electric car sales share
source: International Energy Agency (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, Norway’s share of new car sales that are electric vehicles (EVs) rose to approximately 92%, the highest in the world. 
This is far above most other countries in the world, with only other Nordic countries such as Sweden, Denmark and Finland coming close at around 50-60%.

title: Public social spending as share of GDP
infoDescription:
France allocates over 30% of its GDP to public social expenditure, the highest percentage in the world, ahead of Italy and Austria. 
This share has grown significantly since the 1970s, when it was under 20%, reflecting the expansion of welfare-state components like pensions, healthcare, unemployment benefits, and family supports.

title: Share of the population that is undernourished
infoDescription:
Between 2001 and 2022, Somalia consistently had one the highest rate of undernourishment in the world. 
In 2022, an estimated 51.3% of Somalia’s population was undernourished. 
While this marks an improvement from its peak of nearly 71.4% in 2010, Somalia's undernourishment rate remains the highest, just ahead of Haiti (50.4%). 
The persistently high rates reflect the country’s long-standing challenges with conflict, drought, and food insecurity.

title: Rice production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Indonesia is the fourth-largest rice producer in the world, following India, China, and Bangladesh. 
Its production has grown steadily since the 1960s, supported by government programs aimed at achieving food security and boosting yields. 
By 2023, Indonesia remained one of the top contributors to global rice production, producing more than countries like Vietnam, Thailand, and Myanmar.

title: Deaths in armed conflicts
subtitle: Based on where they occurred
source: Uppsala Conflict Data Program (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Since the start of the full-scale Russian invasion in 2022, Ukraine has seen a sharp rise in conflict-related deaths. 
That year marked the deadliest point in the war, with over 90,000 recorded fatalities. 
Although the number of deaths declined in 2023, and then in 2024, it remained high, reflecting the continued intensity of the conflict.

title: Share of electricity production from coal
infoDescription:
In 2024, coal accounted for just 0.82% of the United Kingdom’s electricity generation, down sharply from a peak of 69% in 1987. 
This places the UK well below the global average of 34%. In the same year, coal still made up over 58% of electricity production in China and around 75% in India. 
The UK’s near-elimination of coal comes ahead of its planned phase-out in 2025 and reflects one of the fastest coal declines among major economies.

title: Wine consumption per person
subtitle: In liters
infoDescription:
In 2020, Denmark recorded a per capita wine consumption of approximately 37 liters, marking a significant rise from the 1960's.
This increasing trend is going against many other countries that have reduced their consumption such as France or Italy.
Despite this rise, France remains the top wine-consuming country globally, ahead of Portugal and Slovenia, with Denmark ranking fifth in 2020.

title: International tourist expenditure abroad
subtitle: In US$ adjusted for inflation
infoDescription:
China has emerged as the world’s top spender on outbound tourism. 
From the early 2000s through the late 2010s, per‑traveller spending rose steeply, peaking just before the pandemic. 
The data shows a sharp drop during 2020–21 due to global travel restrictions, but it did not bounce back in 2022 compared to countries such as the United States or the United Kingdom.

title: Gas production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Iran is the third-largest natural gas producer in the world, after the United States and Russia. 
Its production has remained high over the past two decades, generally ranging between 30 and 40 billion cubic feet per day. 
Iran ranks ahead of China, Canada, and Qatar in global natural gas production.

title: Number of Research & Development researchers
subtitle: Per million people
infoDescription:
In 2022, South Korea had about 9,435 researchers per million people, the highest in the world. 
This is nearly nine times higher than the global average, which stands around 1,050 researchers per million people. 
South Korea far outpaces other innovation leaders such as Germany (5,800), Japan (5,600) and France (5,200).

title: Military spending as a share of GDP
infoDescription:
Israel’s defence spending rose sharply to about 8.8% of its GDP in 2024, doubling its percentage from about 4.4% in 2022. 
Only Ukraine's military spending as a share of GDP is higher at about 34% in 2024.

title: Share of contribution to global warming
source: Jones et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
As of 2023, the United States had contributed about 17.2% of the total human-driven rise in global mean surface temperature - the largest share of any country. 
China ranked second, followed by Russia, Brazil and India.

title: Human Development Index
infoDescription:
Iceland had the highest Human Development Index in 2023, just ahead of Switzerland, Norway and Denmark.
South Sudan was the country with the lowest Human Development Index.

title: Spirits consumption per person
subtitle: In liters of pure alcohol
infoDescription:
In 2020, Russia’s spirits consumption was about 3.17 liters of pure alcohol per year per person aged 15+, down sharply from around 7 liters in 2001. 
This steep decline reflects changing drinking habits and stricter alcohol regulations implemented over the past two decades.

title: Share of the population that is female
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, females made up approximately 36% of United Arab Emirates' population, the second-lowest share in the world after Qatar.
This imbalance is largely due to the large number of male expatriate workers in the country.

title: Private health spending per person
infoDescription:
In 2022, Switzerland’s private health expenditure per person was approximately $6,939, making it the highest in the world. 
The United States is close second with $5,573 per person per year, while Singapore, the Netherlands and Canada follow at around $2,000 to $3,000 per person per year.

title: Average years of schooling
infoDescription:
Germany is the country with the highest mean years of schooling - about 14.3 years of education completed on average by adults aged 25 and older. 
It is the only country exceeding 14 years, ahead of other advanced economies such as Switzerland, the United Stated, Iceland and Canada which are all at an evarge of 13.9 years.

title: Soybean production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Brazil became the world’s largest soybean producer in 2019, overtaking the United States. 
Since then, its production has continued to grow, reaching record levels in 2023, while U.S. output has remained slightly lower. 
Argentina and China follow far behind as the third and fourth largest producers.

title: Nuclear power generation
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
infoDescription:
As of 2024, Japan was the seventh-largest producer of nuclear electricity in the world. 
It used to be the third-largest, but most nuclear activity stopped after the 2011 Fukushima accident, and it has only gradually resumed production since then.

title: Self-reported life satisfaction
subtitle: Out of 10
source: Wellbeing Research Centre (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, Finland had the highest happiness score globally, with a score of 7.74 out of 10. 
Strong social support, trust in institutions, and a high quality of life contribute to its consistent top ranking.
In contrast, Afghanistan, the lowest-ranking country, had a score of 1.36.

title: Armed forces personnel as a share of total population
infoDescription:
North Korea consistently maintained one of the highest shares of armed forces personnel relative to its labor force, with about 5.6% serving in the military in 2020. 
This is significantly above the global average of around 0.3% and only Eritrea had a higher percentage at 6.1% that year, with Israel ranked third with 2%.

title: Death rate from malaria
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
Burkina Faso was once the country with the highest age-standardized malaria death rate in the world. 
However, its malaria mortality rate has decreased in recent years due to improved prevention and treatment efforts, decresing from more than 300 deaths per 100,000 people in 2000 to less than 80 in 2021.
Niger is now the country with the highest malaria death rate in the world.

title: Pumpkin production 🎃
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Happy Halloween! 🎃👻
China is the world’s largest pumpkin producer, producing over 7 million tonnes per year since 2011, and unlike in many countries where pumpkins are mainly used for decoration, they play a key role in Chinese cooking.
India ranks second, having been overtaken by China in 1999.
Other major producers include Russia, Ukraine, and the United States.

title: Orange production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
The United States was the world’s largest orange producer in the 1960s and 1970s, but it was overtaken by Brazil in the 1980s. 
Since the 2000s, U.S. orange production has declined steadily, and by 2023 it had fallen to become only the ninth-largest producer globally. 
Most U.S. oranges are still grown in Florida and California, primarily for juice and fresh consumption.

title: HIV/AIDS deaths
source: Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
South Africa experienced its highest number of AIDS-related deaths in the mid-2000s, with fatalities reaching more than 250,000 per year. 
Since then, the annual toll has declined significantly, dropping to around 50,000 by 2023 - still the highest of any country in the world. 
Nigeria and Mozambique follow, each with approximately 45,000 AIDS-related deaths per year.

title: Share of adults who are overweight or obese
infoDescription:
Tonga has one of the world’s highest proportions of overweight or obese adults, with about 90% of adults exceeding a body mass index of 25 as of 2022. 
This is dramatically higher than the global average, where around 43% of adults are overweight or obese.
Other countries with very high proportions of overweight or obese adults include Chile at 79%, Qatar at 77% or the United States at 72%.

title: Deaths from drug use
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, Canada had the second highest death rates from drug use disorders at approximately 19 deaths per 100,000 people, second only to the United States. 
The U.S. recorded the highest rate with about 32 deaths per 100,000 people. 
By comparison, the global average was just 2 deaths per 100,000 people.

title: Coffee bean production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2023, Vietnam produced approximately 1.96 million tonnes of coffee beans, making it the world’s second-largest coffee producer. 
This accounts for about 18% of global production. 
Brazil remains the largest producer by a wide margin, while Colombia ranks third.

title: Military spending per armed forces personnel
subtitle: in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2020, Australia spent approximately $508,000 per active-duty service member, placing it among the higher spenders globally. 
This is significantly higher than China and India, which spent about $103,000 and $25,000 per service member, respectively, but still below the United States, which spent over $650,000 per service member per year.

title: Share of people practicing open defecation
infoDescription:
India used to have extremely high levels of open defecation - 73% in 2000. 
Over recent decades, it has made dramatic progress, and by 2022 the share fell to around 11% of the population.

title: Share of arable land which is organic
infoDescription:
In 2022, about 20% of Sweden’s arable land was farmed organically, one of the highest shares in the world. 
This places Sweden behind Austria, which leads with over 27%.
In contrast, countries such as the United States, China, and Brazil have very low shares of organic farmland, each at around 0.5%

title: Death rate from road injuries
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
Thailand used to have one of the deadliest road accident death rates in the world, peaking at over 50 deaths per 100,000 people per year in the early 2000s. 
By 2021, its rate had fallen to about 25 per 100,000, still much higher than the global average of about 15 per 100,000 per year.

title: Share of women in parliament
infoDescription:
Yemen remains at the very bottom in terms of female representation in its parliament: in 2024, 0% of parliamentary seats were held by women - the lowest share in the world. 
Only Oman also has a parliament composed entirely of men. In contrast, the global average stood at around 27% in 2024, underscoring Yemen’s persistent exclusion of women from legislative roles.

title: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
infoDescription:
As of 2024, Russia has launched almost 4,000 cumulative objects into space. 
It was overtaken by the United States in 2020, largely due to the surge of SpaceX Starlink satellite launches, while Russia’s launch rate has remained relatively steady.

title: Rice production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
China was the world’s leading rice producer from the 1960s until 2023, when it was surpassed by India. 
For decades, China consistently produced more rice than any other country, reflecting its vast agricultural capacity and central role in global rice supply. 
Although it now ranks second, China’s rice output remains immense and far ahead of other major producers such as Indonesia, Vietnam, and Thailand.

title: Cumulative refugee population by country of asylum
infoDescription:
Since 1980, Iran has steadily hosted one of the world’s largest refugee populations, with a first wave peaking in 1991 at 4.4 million refugees, and a second wave more recently, reaching 3.76 million in 2023. 
That year, Iran was the country hosting the largest number of refugees globally, ahead of Turkey and Germany. 
The vast majority of these refugees come from Afghanistan, a neighboring country that has faced decades of conflict and instability. 
Iran has received large numbers of Afghan refugees since the Soviet invasion in 1979, but arrivals surged again in recent years - especially after the Taliban’s return to power in 2021.

title: Per capita CO₂ emissions from cement
subtitle: In tonnes
source: Global Carbon Budget (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Saudi Arabia saw a dramatic rise in per-capita CO₂ emissions from cement production, increasing from near zero in the 1960s to over 1 tonne per person by the early 2010s. 
During that decade, it became the world's highest per-capita emitter of cement-related CO₂. 
This steep rise reflects the country’s rapid infrastructure expansion and cement-heavy development model over the past several decades.

title: Population
infoDescription:
Nigeria is the sixth most populous country in the world, with a population of over 220 million people. 
It is the most populous country in Africa, far ahead of the next largest African countries. 
Globally, only China, India, the United States, Indonesia, and Pakistan have larger populations.

title: Wealth share of the richest 1%
source: World Inequality Database (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2023, the richest 1 % in the Netherlands held around 14% of the country’s wealth - the lowest share in the world. 
In comparison, the top 1 % in the United States held about 35%, and in the United Kingdom about 21%.
The highest share in the world is 55% in South Africa.

title: Share of cars currently in use that are electric
source: International Energy Agency (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Norway is the world’s leader in electric vehicle adoption, with electric cars making up 32% of its total car fleet in 2024.
This figure is significantly higher than other early adopters such as Iceland and Denmark, where EVs constitute about 18% and 17% of car fleets respectively.

title: Research & Development spending as a share of GDP
infoDescription:
In 2023, Israel allocated approximately 6% of its GDP to research and development, the highest percentage in the world ahead of South Korea and the United States. 
This figure significantly exceeds the global average of 2.6%. 
Historically, Israel's R&D expenditure has been among the highest globally, and has risen from around 2.5% in the 1990s to now about 6%.

title: Number of cattle
infoDescription:
In 2023, the United States had the third largest cattle herd in the world, with around 95 million head of cattle. 
Brazil ranks first with over 230 million head, while India holds the second spot with about 195 million cattle.

title: Deaths in armed conflicts
subtitle: Based on where they occurred
source: Uppsala Conflict Data Program (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Since the outbreak of the Tigray conflict in late 2020, Ethiopia has experienced a sharp increase in conflict-related deaths.
More than 129,000 people died in 2021, and about 165,000 in 2022. 
That's more than in other conflict impacted areas such as Ukraine or Palestine.

title: Coal production per capita
subtitle: Measured in megawatt-hour equivalents
source: OWID based on UN, Gapminder, BP, Etemad & Luciana (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Australia has long been among the world’s top coal-producing nations on a per-person basis, and has risen dramatically since the 1980s.
Its coal production per capita has remained elevated due to both substantial resource extraction and a relatively modest population. 
This persistent prominence reflects Australia’s significant role in global coal markets and its position as one of the world’s largest exporters of coal.

title: Wine consumption per person
subtitle: In liters
infoDescription:
In 2020, France recorded a per capita wine consumption of approximately 46 liters, marking a significant decline from its peak of 171 liters in 1961. 
Despite this decline, France remains the top wine-consuming country globally, ahead of Portugal and Slovenia.

title: Fish and seafood consumption
subtitle: In Kg per person
infoDescription:
In 2022, Iceland maintained its position as the world's leading consumer of fish and seafood, with approximately 85 kg consumed per capita. 
This figure is more than four times the global average of 20.5 kg. Other countries with high fish consumption include the Maldives, South Korea and Portugal.

title: Armed forces personnel
source: International Institute for Strategic Studies (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2016, India surpassed China to become the country with the largest active military personnel. 
India’s armed forces reached approximately 3 million personnel by 2020, while China had around 2.5 million. 
This shift reflects India’s focus on maintaining a robust and sizeable military presence.

title: Patent applications
subtitle: Per million people
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2021, Japan filed around 1,770 patent applications per million people, placing it well above the global average of just over 300 per million, but behind South Korea’s leading rate. 
This represents a decline from its peak in the early 2000s, when Japan was a global leader in patent activity.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Russia is the world’s second-largest oil producer in 2024, ranking behind the United States and just before Saudi Arabia. 
Its production has ranged between 9 and 11 million barrels per day over the past two decades, making it a major contributor to global oil supply.

title: Turkey meat production 🦃
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Happy thanksgiving! 🦃
The United States is the largest producer of turkey meat in the world, producing about 2.5 million tonnes in 2021. 
This is roughly five times more than the second-largest producer, Brazil. Germany, Poland, and Italy follow as the next major producers.
The country of Turkey is the 14th largest producer, producing about 50 times less turkey meat than the United States.

title: Number of women who die from maternal conditions
infoDescription:
In Nigeria, maternal deaths (death of women during or shortly after pregnancy) increased from about 52,000 in 1985 to over 74,000 in 2023. 
This rise makes Nigeria the country with the highest number of maternal deaths in the world, driven by limited access to quality healthcare and high fertility rates.
In contrast, India's maternal deaths fell dramatically from about 180,000 deaths in 1985 to less than 20,000 in 2023.

title: Sunflower seed production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2023, Ukraine was the world’s second-largest sunflower seed producer just behind Russia, but ahead of Argentina.
Its production fell in 2022 following Russia’s invasion, which reduced the amount of arable land that could be safely cultivated.

title: Deaths from homicide
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
source: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In the 1990s, Colombia had the highest homicide rate in the world, with levels exceeding 80 deaths per 100,000 people. 
Since then, the country has seen a major decline, with the rate falling to around 25 per 100,000 in 2023. 
This is still about five times the global average of about 5.2 per 100,000.

title: Coffee bean production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2023, Indonesia produced approximately 760,000 tonnes of coffee beans, making it the world’s third-largest coffee producer. 
This accounts for about 7% of global production. 
Brazil remains the largest producer by a wide margin, followed by Vietnam in second place.

title: Spirits consumption per person
subtitle: In liters of pure alcohol
infoDescription:
Latvia recorded an extremely high level of spirits consumption per person, reaching approximately 5.7 litres of pure alcohol from spirits alone in 2020 - the highest in the world.
It is followed by Lithuania and Thailand.

title: Methane emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
China is the world’s largest emitter of methane, largely because of its coal-mining and fossil-fuel sectors. 
Its methane output from the energy sector alone reached around 25 million tonnes in 2024 - more than any other country. 
Other major methane-emitting countries include India and the United States.

title: Imports of tomatoes
subtitle: In million tonnes - methodological change from 2010 onwards
infoDescription:
The United Kingdom is the world's second-largest importers of tomatoes, only behind Germany. 
Most tomatoes entering the UK are supplied by countries like Italy, Spain, Morocco and the Netherlands.
Note: the FAO applied a methodological change from the year 2010 onwards.

title: Number of live sheep per person
infoDescription:
In recent decades, New Zealand’s ratio of sheep to people has fallen sharply - from around 20 sheep per person at its peak to roughly 4.7 sheep per person today. 
This decline reflects a reduction in the national flock due to changes in farming practices, lower global wool prices, and shifts in land use, even though sheep still outnumber humans overall.
Mongolia is now the country with the most live sheep per person, with about 8.5 per person in 2023.

title: Share of electricity production from nuclear
infoDescription:
In recent years, Belarus has significantly increased its reliance on nuclear power for electricity generation. 
The share of electricity produced from nuclear sources rose from 0% in 2019 to 36.5% in 2024, following the commencement of operations at the Astravets Nuclear Power Plant.
France remains the most nuclear dependent country in the world with 68% of its electricity produced from nuclear.

title: Rye production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Germany is currently the world’s largest rye producer, harvesting over 3.1 million tonnes in 2023. 
Rye production is concentrated in northern and eastern Germany, where the climate and soil are ideal. 
The country uses rye mainly for bread, animal feed, and some industrial applications, and it exports part of its harvest to neighboring European countries.

title: Fertility rate: births per woman
infoDescription:
Somalia had the highest fertility rate in the world in 2023, with women having an average of about 6.1 children. 
This rate has remained consistently high compared to other countries, even as global fertility has declined significantly over recent decades. 
Other African countries also have very high fertility rates, such as Chad, Niger and the Democratic Republic of Congo. 
In contrast, highly developed regions like South Korea, Hong Kong, and Taiwan recorded some of the world’s lowest fertility rates, with fewer than 1 child per woman.

title: Raspberry production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Mexico is the second-largest raspberry producer globally, with roughly 190,000 tonnes in 2023, just behind Russia. 
The berries are mainly grown in the states of Michoacán, Jalisco, and Baja California, and a significant share is exported, especially to the United States.
Mexico only grew about 12 tonnes in 1992, rising up to almost 200,000 tonnes in 2023.

title: Hospital beds
subtitle: Per 1,000 people
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2021, South Korea had 12.75 hospital beds per 1,000 people, the highest rate in the world. 
This is nearly three times the global average of 4.44 beds per 1,000 people. 
Over the past four decades, South Korea’s hospital bed capacity has steadily increased - from 1.7 beds per 1,000 people in 1980 to the current level. 
In contrast, most countries have seen a decline in hospital beds over the same period. 
Japan follows closely with 12.72 beds per 1,000 people in 2021.

title: Copper production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Chile is the world’s largest copper producer, accounting for about 23% of global mined output in 2023. 
Its industry has remained dominant for decades thanks to vast reserves and large-scale operations, keeping it well ahead of all other countries such as Peru and the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

title: Palm oil production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Malaysia is the second-largest palm-oil producer globally, producing about a quarter of total world output. 
While it trails Indonesia substantially, Malaysia remains a key supplier and global competitor in palm-oil production.

title: Per capita electricity generation
subtitle: In kilowatt-hours per person
infoDescription:
Iceland generates more electricity per capita than any other country in the world, by a very large margin. 
Its exceptionally high per-person output comes from abundant hydro and geothermal resources, a small population, and the presence of energy-intensive industries such as aluminum smelting.
The countries that follow are Norway in second place and Bahrain in third, both far behind Iceland’s extraordinary levels.

title: Sugar cane production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
India is the second-largest sugar‑cane producer in the world, following Brazil. 
It produces nearly 500 million tonnes per year, far more than other major producers such as China, Thailand, and Pakistan.

title: Deaths in armed conflicts
subtitle: Based on where they occurred
source: Uppsala Conflict Data Program (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 1994, Rwanda experienced a surge in conflict-related deaths, with over 750,000 fatalities recorded in a single year. 
This tragic spike corresponds to the Rwandan genocide, an ethnic massacre primarily targeting the Tutsi minority by extremist Hutu militias. 
The genocide unfolded over roughly 100 days, from April to July 1994.

title: Pepper production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Vietnam is the world’s top producer of pepper, supplying roughly 40% of global output. 
Its 2023 production remains well ahead of all other countries, such as Brazil, Burkina Faso and Indonesia.

title: Known crude oil reserves
subtitle: In billion cubic meters
source: US Energy Information Administration (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Venezuela has the largest known crude oil reserves in the world, totaling over 48 billion cubic meters, which accounts for roughly 18% of global reserves. 
Its known reserves expanded extremely rapidly between 2010 and 2013, rising from 15.8 to 47.3 billion cubic meters, an increase of roughly 200%, largely due to the formal certification of the Orinoco Belt’s extra-heavy crude. 
Saudi Arabia holds the second-largest reserves, and Iran ranks third.

title: Suicide rate
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, Russia's suicide rate was approximately 21.4 per 100,000 people, a significant decline from over 50 per 100,000 in 2000. 
While Russia used to have the highest rate globally, Lesotho had the highest suicide rate in 2021 at about 30 per 100,000, still much lower than Russia’s peak levels two decades earlier.

title: Military spending per armed forces personnel
subtitle: in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2020, the United States spent over $650,000 per active-duty service member, significantly more than other major military powers. 
This figure was approximately 50% higher than the United Kingdom, more than double Germany’s spending per personnel, about six times China’s, and more than twenty times that of Brazil or India.

title: Pineapple production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription: Costa Rica is the third-largest pineapple producer globally, producing about 2.94 million tonnes in 2023 — very close to the Philippines and just behind Indonesia.

title: Share of children who are overweight or obese
infoDescription:
Australia’s share of children who are overweight reached approximately 26.4% in 2024, the highest rate in the world, and significantly higher than its 7.3% rate in 2000.
This places Australia significantly above the global average, which is closer to 5.5%.

title: Olive oil production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription: Italy is the second-largest olive oil producer, consistently behind Spain since 2000, but still well above other Mediterranean producers like Greece, Tunisia, and Morocco.

title: Average years of schooling
infoDescription:
Niger is the country with the lowest mean years of education in the world, with on average just 1.4 years of education completed by adults aged 25 and older. 
This figure falls below other African countries, such as Mali and Somalia, and well below the global average of 8.8 years.

title: Population
infoDescription:
China’s population, according to UN data, reached its peak around 2022–2023 and has now begun to decline after decades of steady growth. 
For much of modern history, China was the most populous country in the world, but India overtook it in 2023. 
Population growth in China was rapid after 1950, yet has slowed sharply in recent decades as fertility rates fell below replacement levels. 
Projections suggest this decline will continue, marking a significant demographic shift for the country.

title: Number of live pigs per person
infoDescription:
Denmark has more pigs than people, with about 2 pigs for every 1 resident. 
This high ratio reflects the scale of pig farming in the country, where a relatively small human population manages a very large pig population.
Spain is second with a ratio of 0.7 pig per person in 2023.

title: Exports of lemons and limes
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Argentina is the world’s largest exporter of lemons and limes, ahead of Mexico and South Africa.
Its success reflects a combination of favorable growing conditions, large-scale production, and well-organized export infrastructure, making Argentina the dominant supplier in the global lemon and lime market. 
A key destination for these exports is the United States, along with European countries such as the Netherlands, Spain, and Italy.

title: Gold production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
South Africa was once the dominant gold producer in the world, especially during the 20th century, with annual output peaking at around 700 tonnes and accounting for about 60% of global production. 
Over recent decades its gold production has declined steeply — falling to around 100 tonnes in 2023 — largely because its major deposits have matured, ore grades have fallen, mines have become deeper and more expensive to operate, and cheaper gold sources have emerged elsewhere. 
As of 2023, the top three gold producers globally are China in first place, followed by Russia and Australia, each with significantly higher annual output than South Africa, but lower than 1980s South Africa.

title: Electric car sales share
source: International Energy Agency (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, electric vehicles (EVs) made up 42% of new car sales in Iceland, a notable decline from the 70% range seen over the previous three years. 
This contrasts with broader global trends, where EV adoption continues to rise - especially in Nordic countries, with Norway leading at 94% in 2024.

title: Corn production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
The United States remains the world’s largest producer of corn, with around 400 million tonnes annually. 
Its massive “Corn Belt” and highly efficient agriculture sector keep U.S. output far ahead of other countries such as China and Brazil.

title: Deaths from drug use
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, the United Kingdom had the third highest death rates from drug use disorders at approximately 8.4 deaths per 100,000 people per year, behind the United States and Canada. 
The U.S. recorded the highest rate with about 32 deaths per 100,000 people. 
By comparison, the global average was just 2 deaths per 100,000 people.

title: Hazelnut production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Turkey is the world’s largest hazelnut producer, producing roughly six times more than the second-largest producer, Italy. 
Its favorable climate and extensive cultivation areas allow it to dominate the global hazelnut market.

title: Uranium production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Canada is the world’s second-largest uranium producer, accounting for roughly 21% of global mine output in 2023. 
It ranks well behind Kazakhstan’s dominant position but remains ahead of other major producers such as Australia and Namibia.

title: Cumulative refugee population by country of asylum
infoDescription:
Germany has consistently hosted one of the largest refugee populations in Europe. 
In 2023, the country was home to approximately 2.75 million refugees, marking a steady rise from just under 200,000 in 2013. 
Over the years, Germany has received refugees from various regions, including Syria, Afghanistan, Iraq, and more recently, Ukraine.

title: Date production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription: Egypt is the world’s leading producer of dates, ahead of Saudi Arabia and Algeria, producing over 1.8 million tonnes annually and supplying a major portion of the global market.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Saudi Arabia is one of the world’s leading oil producers, consistently ranking near the top alongside the United States and Russia. 
Its production has generally ranged between 9 and 12 million barrels per day over the past two decades, making it a key player in the global oil market.

title: Number of live horses per person
infoDescription:
Mongolia has the highest horses‑per‑capita ratio in the world, with about 1.4 horses per person. 
This reflects the country’s nomadic culture and reliance on horses for transportation, herding, and traditional livelihoods, making horses an integral part of daily life.

title: Coconut production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
The Philippines is the world’s second-largest coconut producer, just behind Indonesia and ahead of India. 
It produces around 15 million tonnes annually, making it a key supplier in the global coconut market.

title: Gas production per capita
subtitle: In kilowatt-hours per person
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Qatar leads the world in natural gas production per capita, producing far more gas per person than any other country. 
The second- and third-highest per-capita producers in 2024 are Brunei and Norway, respectively.

title: Cherry production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Chile has emerged in recent years as a major force in global cherry production, now ranking as the second-largest producer in the world, behind Turkey and ahead of the United States.
Its growth in cherry output reflects a rapid expansion of orchards and rising yields, allowing it to become a key supplier in global markets.

title: Beer consumption per person
subtitle: In liters
infoDescription:
Czechia was the largest per-capita beer consumer in the world in 2019, with people drinking over 130 litres of beer per person that year. 
This outpaced other countries such as Austria, Poland and Germany.

title: Proportion of labor force who are women
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Yemen’s share of women in the labor force was just approximately 7.4% in 2024. 
This is dramatically below the global average of roughly 40%. 
Historically, participation peaked at about 23.6% in 1999, before entering a steep decline in the 2000s. 
The recent low levels largely reflect persistent structural barriers to women’s economic participation in Yemen.
Only Afghanistan had a lower percentage in 2024.

title: Artichoke production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Italy used to be the world’s top artichoke producer for decades, but it was overtaken by Egypt in 2021. 
Its production remains far higher than most other countries, with regions like Puglia, Sicily, and Lazio continuing to play a leading role in cultivation.

title: Fertility rate: births per woman
infoDescription:
South Korea recorded one of the world’s lowest fertility rate in 2023, with women having an average of just 0.7 children, down sharply from around 6 children per woman in the 1950s. 
This rate stands well below the global average of 2.3 births per woman in 2023. 
While nations like Somalia and Chad continue to exhibit fertility rates above 6 children per woman, South Korea remains at the opposite extreme, underscoring the stark contrast between rapid development and persistent demographic decline.

title: Exports of banana
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Ecuador has been the world’s largest exporter of bananas for decades, ahead of Guatemala, Philippines and Colombia.,
Its dominance is supported by a favorable tropical climate, large-scale banana farms, and efficient export infrastructure, allowing it to consistently supply vast quantities to markets around the globe.

title: Share of electricity production from nuclear
infoDescription:
Since the mid-1980s, nuclear energy was Japan’s largest electricity source, reaching about 30% of the mix before 2011. 
Following the Fukushima disaster in 2011, nuclear power production plummeted to nearly 0% by 2014, as reactors were shut down. 
Reactors have slowly returned to service since then. In 2024, nuclear accounted for 8.3% of Japan's electricity.

title: Armed forces personnel
source: International Institute for Strategic Studies (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
North Korea had the third-largest active military in the world in 2020, despite its relatively small population and land area. 
Only India and China have larger armies, with North Korea’s forces estimated at around 1.47 million personnel, reflecting its focus on maintaining a substantial standing army.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
The United States is the world’s top oil producer, with production consistently exceeding 11 million barrels per day in recent years. 
Its output has grown steadily over the past two decades, driven largely by the expansion of shale oil and technological advancements. 
The U.S. consistently outproduces other major oil producers, including Saudi Arabia and Russia, maintaining its position at the top of global oil production.

title: Share of contribution to global warming
source: Jones et al. (2025) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
As of 2024, China had contributed about 13% of the total human-driven rise in global mean surface temperature, making it the second-largest contributor after the United States. 
Although China’s historical share is smaller, its emissions have risen sharply in recent decades, surpassing those of Russia, Brazil and India.

title: Deaths in armed conflicts
subtitle: Based on where they occurred
source: Uppsala Conflict Data Program (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
More than 40,000 people died in Eritrea in 1999 as well as in 2000 during the Eritrea-Ethiopia war. 
The conflict, sparked by a disputed border area, led to intense fighting and widespread destruction, resulting in heavy military and civilian casualties. 
Despite a ceasefire agreement in late 2000, the war’s aftermath left deep social and economic scars, with lasting impacts on the region’s stability and development.

title: Mango production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
India is the world’s largest producer of mangoes by a wide margin, supplying a substantial share of global production - roughly 42% of all mangoes grown worldwide. 
Its long history of mango cultivation and favourable climate make it the dominant force in the global mango market, far ahead of other mango-producing countries such as Indonesia or China.

title: Number of live camels
infoDescription:
Chad has more camels than any other country, with over 10 million live camels, making it the largest camel population in the world. 
For context, there is about 20 million people living in chad, so one camel for two inhabitants.
Camels are essential to Chad’s traditional livelihoods, providing transportation, milk, and meat, and supporting nomadic and rural communities across arid and semi‑arid regions. 
Other countries with large camel populations include Somalia and Sudan, though they are far behind Chad.

title: Silver production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Mexico is the world’s largest silver producer, accounting for about a quarter of global mine output in 2023, well ahead of any other country. 
China and Peru follow as the second- and third-largest producers, each contributing roughly one-eighth of global silver production, but still far behind Mexico’s dominant position.

title: Share of electricity production from wind
infoDescription:
In 2024, wind power accounted for almost 60% of Denmark’s electricity generation, keeping it the global leader in wind energy share. 
Denmark was one of the first countries to adopt wind power as part of its energy mix in the late 1980s and has consistently maintained the highest wind electricity share worldwide. 
This stands in sharp contrast to the global average, where wind power accounts for just around 8% of electricity generation. 
Other leading countries include Ireland and the United Kingdom, but none have matched Denmark’s sustained dominance in wind energy.

title: Exports of apples
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Poland is the world’s largest exporter of apples, shipping about 3.73 million tonnes in 2022. 
This places it ahead of China and Turkey, making it the top supplier of apples on the global market.

title: Bauxite production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Australia is the largest bauxite producer in the world, ahead of Guinea and China, supplying about 24.5% of global mined output. 
Bauxite from Australia is primarily used to produce aluminum, which is essential for construction, transportation, packaging, electrical wiring, and a wide range of consumer goods.

title: Apricot production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Turkey is the world’s leading producer of apricots, well ahead of other countries. 
Its high production is supported by favorable climate and extensive apricot-growing regions, especially around Malatya, often called the “apricot capital” of the country.
Other major apricot producers include Uzbekistan and Iran, which follow Turkey but produce substantially less.

title: Rice production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Bangladesh is the third-largest rice producer in the world, behind India and China. 
Since the 1960s, its rice production has increased dramatically due to expanded cultivation and improved yields. 
By 2023, Bangladesh firmly held its position among the top three producers, producing more rice than countries such as Indonesia, Vietnam, and Thailand.

title: Death rate from poisoning
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
Russia used to have one of the highest death rates from poisoning in the 2000s, alongside countries such as Moldova and Ukraine.
By 2021, its rate had decreased dramatically but still ranked among the top 10 countries in the world with the highest death rate from poisoning.

title: Cumulative refugee population by country of origin
infoDescription:
Afghanistan has long been one of the world’s leading countries of origin for refugees, with the number reaching over 6.4 million in 2023. 
This dramatic recent rise reflects ongoing instability, particularly following the Taliban’s return to power in 2021. 
Previous waves of displacement were driven by decades of conflict, including the Soviet invasion in 1979, civil war in the 1990s, and the U.S.-led intervention post-2001.

title: Number of cattle
infoDescription:
In 2023, Pakistan had about 55 million head of cattle, making it one of the top cattle producers globally. 
This is significantly lower than Brazil’s 230 million or India's 195 million, but places Pakistan among the world’s leading countries.
It significantly increased its herd from about 22 million in 2000, and overtook countries such as Argentina.

title: Nitrogen fertilizer use per hectare of cropland
subtitle: In kg
infoDescription:
Egypt applies more nitrogen fertilizer per hectare of cropland than any other country, at around 328 kg of nitrogen per hectare in 2022.
This is a significant rise from about 75 kg per hectare in 1961.
This high usage reflects Egypt’s agricultural system, where most arable land is irrigated along the Nile and often supports multiple cropping cycles per year, driving up fertilizer demand for cereals and vegetables.

title: Wine production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2022, Spain was the world’s third largest wine producer, with production reaching approximately 3.6 million tonnes. 
This placed it behind Italy, which produced about 5.4 million tonnes, and behind France, with around 4.6 million tonnes. 
The United States followed in fourth place, producing roughly 2.4 million tonnes.

title: Nitrogen oxides emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Over the past decades, the United States saw a substantial decline in NOₓ emissions per year.
This downward trend reflects stricter air‑quality regulations, cleaner power generation, and improvements in vehicle and industrial pollution controls. 
NOₓ levels peaked in 1979. In contrast, China's peak was in 2012.

title: Chicken meat production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Brazil is the world’s second-largest producer of chicken meat, with output reaching about 14.83 million tonnes in 2023. 
This places it behind the United States at around 20 million tonnes, and slightly ahead of China at 14.8 million tonnes. 
Brazil’s chicken production has expanded drastically in the 1990s, making it a dominant global supplier and consistently among the top three producers.

title: Cumulative refugee population by country of asylum
infoDescription:
Since the early 2010s, Turkey has become one of the world’s largest hosts of refugees, with numbers rising sharply following the outbreak of the Syrian civil war in 2011. 
By 2015, Turkey was hosting over 2.5 million refugees, and this number continued to climb, reaching nearly 3.8 million in 2021. 
The vast majority of refugees in Turkey are Syrians fleeing conflict, though smaller numbers also come from Afghanistan, Iraq, and other neighboring states.
Only Iran was hosting more refugees than Turkey in 2024.

title: Exports of milk
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
New Zealand ranks second globally in milk exports as of 2021, driven by a dairy sector that plays a central role in the country’s economy. 
Despite its smaller population and land area, its export-oriented production model keeps it among the top exporters worldwide, only behind Germany.

title: Armed forces personnel as a share of total population
infoDescription:
Eritrea saw a sharp increase in armed forces personnel as a share of its labor force between 1998 and 1999, rising from 2.2% to 9%. 
This significant jump reflects the country’s military mobilisation during the border conflict with Ethiopia. 
By 2021, Eritrea maintained the highest percentage globally, with 6.1% of its labor force serving in the military, far above the global average of around 0.3%. 
Eritrea’s militarisation level remains notably higher than other countries, including North Korea at 5.6% and Israel at 2% in 2021.

title: Cobalt production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
The Democratic Republic of the Congo dominates global cobalt production, supplying around 70% of the world’s mined cobalt—far more than any other country. 
This overwhelming lead comes from rich cobalt deposits in its copper belt, where cobalt is produced mainly as a by-product of copper mining, while other producers such as Indonesia and Russia contribute much smaller shares.

title: Almond production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Australia is a notable new player in global almond production - ranking as the third-largest producer worldwide as of 2023. 
Australian almonds now represent a significant share of global almond supply, and the country has emerged as a strong alternative to traditional producers such as the United States and Spain.

title: Share of women in ministerial positions
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
As of 2024, Nicaragua has the highest share of women in ministerial positions in the world, with women holding around two-thirds of all cabinet posts. 
This puts Nicaragua well ahead of countries such as Finland and Iceland, making it a global outlier in female representation at the ministerial level.

title: Pistachio production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Iran was once the world’s leading producer of pistachios, holding the top position for many years. 
Over time, however, its production growth was overtaken by the United States, which is now the largest producer globally. 
Today, Iran typically ranks second in pistachio production, followed by Turkey in third place.

title: Methane emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
India is the second-largest methane emitter in the world, trailing only China. 
Its high methane emissions stem largely from agriculture (especially livestock and rice cultivation) and other human-driven sources.

title: Coal production per capita
subtitle: Measured in megawatt-hour equivalents
source: OWID based on UN, Gapminder, BP, Etemad & Luciana (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Mongolia is one of the world’s leading coal producers on a per-person basis, with coal production per capita reaching exceptionally high levels in recent years. 
Output rose sharply in the 2000s as the country expanded mining operations, placing it well ahead of major producers like China, India, or the United States on a per-capita basis. 
Mongolia’s prominence reflects its abundant coal reserves relative to its small population, making it one of the most coal-intensive economies globally.

title: Corn production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
China is the second‑largest corn producer globally behind the United States, producing nearly 290 million tonnes in 2023. 
Most of this production is used domestically - mainly for animal feed and food supply - reflecting China’s huge population and demand.

title: Cashew nut production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Ivory Coast is the world’s largest producer of cashew nuts as of 2023, accounting for more than a quarter of global production.
Its output surpasses that of India and Vietnam, making cashews one of the country’s most important agricultural commodities. 
Cashew production in Ivory Coast has grown significantly since the 1990s, reflecting expanded cultivation and strong export demand.

title: Public social spending as share of GDP
infoDescription:
Austria allocates 31.6% of its GDP to public social expenditure, the highest percentage in the world as of 2024, ahead of Finland and France. 
This marks the first time Austria has led globally in social spending as a share of GDP, after more than two decades in which France consistently held the top position.

title: Wine production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2022, France was the world’s second largest wine producer, with production reaching approximately 4.6 million tonnes. 
This placed it behind Italy, which produced about 5.4 million tonnes, and ahead of Spain, with around 3.6 million tonnes. 
The United States followed in fourth place, producing roughly 2.4 million tonnes.

title: Population
infoDescription:
The United States’ population has grown steadily since 1950 and is now above 340 million people. 
While growth continues, the pace has slowed compared to earlier decades, reflecting declining fertility rates. 
Projections suggest that the U.S. population will keep rising throughout most of the century, though at a much slower rate than countries like India. 
Today, it is the third most populous country in the world, behind only India and China.

title: Foreign aid given as a share of national income
infoDescription:
Norway is the country in the world that gives the largest share of its national income to foreign aid, at just over 1% in 2024.
This places it ahead of Luxembourg, Sweden and Denmark and well above the United Kingdom at about 0.5% and the United States at 0.23%, underscoring how exceptional Norway’s contribution is relative to the size of its economy.

title: Coffee bean production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
In 2023, Colombia produced approximately 680,000 tonnes of coffee beans, making it the world’s fourth-largest coffee producer.
This accounts for about 6% of global production.
Brazil remains the largest producer by a wide margin, followed by Vietnam in second place, and Indonesia in third.

title: Share of electricity production from nuclear
infoDescription:
Nuclear power once played a major role in Germany’s electricity generation, supplying around 30% of the total mix through the early 2000s. 
Following the 2011 Fukushima disaster in Japan, Germany accelerated its nuclear phase-out policy. 
It reached 0% in 2024, marking the complete shutdown of all nuclear reactors. 
Unlike countries that have restarted or expanded nuclear programs, Germany has fully exited nuclear power as part of its broader energy transition.

title: Gross domestic product (GDP)
subtitle: In US dollars adjusted for inflation
source: Eurostat, OECD, IMF, and World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
China is the world’s largest economy as of 2024, with GDP at roughly $33.6 trillion, reflecting its sustained rapid expansion over recent decades. 
It became number one in 2016, when China reached about $21.9 trillion and overtook the United States at roughly $21.1 trillion, marking a pivotal shift in global economic weight.

title: Deaths in armed conflicts
subtitle: Based on where they occurred
source: Uppsala Conflict Data Program (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Conflict-related deaths in Afghanistan showed a consistent rise from the mid-1990s onward, reflecting the country’s prolonged instability. 
In 1995, annual fatalities were about 5,000, but this number steadily increased over the following decades. 
It peaked in 2021 with more than 36,000 recorded deaths, and abruptely declined the next year.

title: Cumulative refugee population by country of origin
infoDescription:
Ukraine has seen a dramatic rise in refugee numbers in recent years, with the population of refugees originating from the country jumping from around 27,000 in 2021 to almost 6 million in 2023. 
This surge directly followed the full-scale Russian invasion in early 2022, which triggered widespread displacement across Europe. 
As of 2023, Ukraine had the third-highest number of refugees by country of origin, behind only Afghanistan and Syria.

title: Cumulative refugee population by country of origin
infoDescription:
Syria has been the world’s largest source of refugees for much of the past decade, with the number reaching over 6.3 million in 2023, just behind Afghanistan. 
This massive displacement was triggered by the outbreak of the Syrian civil war in 2011.

title: Nuclear power generation
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
infoDescription:
As of 2024, China was the second-largest producer of nuclear electricity in the world, only behind the United States. 
Other major nuclear energy producers include France, Russia, and South Korea.

title: Share of electricity production from nuclear
infoDescription:
Until 2009, nuclear power played a central role in Lithuania’s electricity generation, supplying around 75% of the country's electricity in the late 2000s. 
This came entirely from the Ignalina Nuclear Power Plant, Lithuania’s only nuclear facility. 
Following the shutdown of its two reactors, the country’s nuclear share dropped to 0% by 2010, where it has remained.

title: Share of electricity production from coal
infoDescription:
In 2024, coal accounted for about 82% of South Africa’s electricity generation, far exceeding the global average of 34%. 
This places South Africa among the countries most dependent on coal for electricity, with only Botswana, Kosovo, and Mongolia reporting higher shares. 
The country’s heavy reliance on coal underscores ongoing challenges in diversifying its energy mix and reducing carbon emissions.

title: Number of cattle
infoDescription:
In 2023, Argentina had about 54 million head of cattle, making it one of the top cattle producers globally. 
This is significantly lower than Brazil’s 230 million or India's 195 million, but places Argentina among the world’s leading countries.

title: Deaths from drug use
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, the Iceland had the fourth highest death rates from drug use disorders at approximately 8.3 deaths per 100,000 people per year, behind the United States, Canada and the United Kingdom. 
The U.S. recorded the highest rate with about 32 deaths per 100,000 people. 
By comparison, the global average was just 2 deaths per 100,000 people.

title: Academic papers published
subtitle: Per million people
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Switzerland ranks first globally in scientific publications per capita. In 2020, it published 2,671 academic papers per million people.
It has held the top position consistently since 1996, reflecting long-term investment in research and higher education. 
In 2020, Denmark ranked second and Sweden was third.
The global average in 2020 was 373 publications per million people.

title: Sunflower seed production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription: In 2023, Russia was the world’s largest sunflower seed producer with more than 18 million tonnes, followed by Ukraine with about 13 million tonnes and Argentina with 5 million tonnes.

title: Number of cattle
infoDescription:
In 2023, India had the second largest cattle herd in the world, with around 195 million head of cattle. 
Brazil ranks first with over 230 million head, while the United States holds the third spot with about 95 million cattle.

title: Population
infoDescription:
Indonesia is the fourth most populous country in the world, with a population of over 280 million people. 
It is the largest country by population in Southeast Asia, and the only one in the region to rank among the global top five. 
The only countries with more people are India, China, and the United States, placing Indonesia just behind them in overall population size.

title: Average years of schooling
infoDescription:
In 1990, the United States had the highest average years of schooling, with adults completing around 13 years on average - well ahead of most other countries at the time.
However, over the following decades, countries like Germany and Switzerland steadily closed the gap. 
By 2002, Germany had surpassed the U.S., and by 2023, the U.S. ranked third, behind both Germany and Switzerland.

title: Population
infoDescription:
Pakistan is the fifth most populous country in the world, with a population exceeding 240 million people. 
It is the most populous country in South Asia after India and ranks just behind Indonesia globally. 
Only China, India, the United States, and Indonesia have larger populations.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Canada is the fourth-largest oil producer in the world, after the United States, Saudi Arabia, and Russia. 
Its production has steadily increased over the past two decades, keeping it well ahead of other significant producers like Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, and Venezuela.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Iran is the fifth-largest oil producer in the world in 2024, after the United States, Saudi Arabia, Russia, and Canada. 
Its production has fluctuated over the past two decades due to sanctions and geopolitical factors, generally ranging between 3 and 4 million barrels per day. 
Despite these challenges, Iran continues to produce more than other significant producers like Iraq, Venezuela, and the United Arab Emirates.

title: Oil production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Iraq is the sixth-largest oil producer in the world in 2024, after the United States, Saudi Arabia, Russia, Canada, and Iran. 
Its production has steadily increased over the past two decades, reaching around 4–5 million barrels per day in recent years. 
Iraq produces more than countries like Venezuela and the United Arab Emirates, securing its position among the top oil-producing nations globally.

title: Share of electricity production from solar
infoDescription:
In 2024, solar energy accounted for 24.62% of Hungary's electricity generation, marking a significant increase from just 0.24% a decade earlier. 
Between 2018 and 2024, Hungary's solar share grew from about 2% to over 24%, reflecting rapid expansion. 
This surge positions Hungary among Europe's top solar producers, surpassing countries like Italy and Spain in solar electricity share.

title: Rice production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Vietnam is the fifth-largest rice producer in the world, ranking just behind India, China, Bangladesh, and Indonesia. 
Since the 1960s, its rice production has grown significantly, transforming the country into one of the leading players in both rice cultivation and exports. 
By 2023, Vietnam continued to produce more rice than other major producers such as Thailand, Myanmar, and the Philippines.

title: Rice production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
India overtook China in 2023 as the top rice producer in the world. 
Since the 1960s and through many decades, India’s rice production trailed China, but in 2023 its output slightly exceeded China’s. 
Despite this shift, India remains closely matched with China and still far ahead of other major producers like Bangladesh, Indonesia, and Vietnam.

title: Gas production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
The United States is the world’s largest natural gas producer, consistently leading since the early 2000s. 
By 2024, it produced nearly 100 billion cubic feet per day, more than Russia and Iran combined. 
This dominance comes from the rapid expansion of shale gas extraction and a robust infrastructure, making the U.S. a central player in global gas markets.

title: Gas production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Canada is the fifth-largest natural gas producer in the world, after the United States, Russia, and China, and just ahead of Qatar. 
Its production has remained relatively stable over the past two decades, generally ranging between 15 and 20 billion cubic feet per day. 
Canada ranks ahead of other significant producers such as Saudi Arabia and Australia.

title: Gas production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Russia is the second-largest natural gas producer in the world, having been surpassed by the United States in the 2010s. 
Its production has remained high over the past two decades, often exceeding 70 billion cubic feet per day. 
The third and fourth-largest producers are Iran and China, respectively.

title: Gas production
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Qatar is the sixth-largest natural gas producer in the world, after the United States, Russia, Iran, China, and Canada. 
Its production has remained relatively stable over the past two decades, generally ranging between 15 and 20 billion cubic feet per day. 
Qatar ranks ahead of other significant producers such as Saudi Arabia and Australia.

title: Death rate from malnutrition
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
Somalia experienced a severe famine from 2010 to 2012, causing an estimated 258,000 deaths, including a large number of children. 
The crisis was driven by drought, failed harvests, and ongoing conflict, and it led to a sharp spike in malnutrition-related deaths during that period.

title: Gas production per capita
subtitle: In kilowatt-hours per person
source: Energy Institute (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Brunei is the world’s second top natural gas producers per capita, having been the highest in the 1980s and 1990s. 
In 2024, it ranked second, just behind Qatar, with Norway in third place.

title: Deaths from homicide
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
source: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Ecuador’s homicide rate surged sharply in recent years, rising from about 5.8 per 100,000 people in 2018 to 45.7 in 2023.
