Only Jamaica had a higher homicide rate in 2023 at 49.3 homicide per 100,000 people.

title: Cocaine use disorder death rate
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
The United States has the highest age-standardized cocaine use disorder death rate in the world, at approximately 3.2 deaths per 100,000 people in 2021. 
Canada ranks second, with a rate of around 2.2 deaths per 100,000, and Guatemala is third with a much lower rate of 0.65 deaths per 100,000.

title: Death rate from malaria
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
Niger has the highest age-standardized malaria death rate in the world, with approximately 132 deaths per 100,000 people in 2021. 
It is followed by other African countries such as Sierra Leone and the Central African Republic.

title: Chicken meat production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
The United States is the world’s largest producer of chicken meat, with output reaching about 20 million tonnes in 2023. 
Brazil is the second-largest producer at 14.83 million tonnes, and China is the third-largest at 14.8 million tonnes. 
U.S. chicken production has grown steadily since the 1960s, expanding more than seven fold, and it has consistently remained at the top globally.

title: Death rate from poisoning
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, Moldova had the highest age-standardized death rate from poisoning, at about 4 deaths per 100,000 people. 
Lesotho ranked second, and Zimbabwe third, both with slightly lower rates.
During the 2000s, Russia and Ukraine had some of the highest poisoning death rates alongside Moldova, but have since declined faster than Moldova.

title: Industry jobs as a share of total employment
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2023, Qatar's industrial sector employed approximately 39.4% of the workforce. 
This marks a significant decline from the maximum of 59.6% in 2008, indicating a trend towards deindustrialization. 
Globally, the average share of employment in industry is around 23.7%, highlighting Qatar's relatively high industrial employment compared to many other nations.

title: Armed forces personnel
source: International Institute for Strategic Studies (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
China’s active military personnel have declined significantly over the past few decades, from more than 4 million in the 1990s to about 2.5 million in 2020. 
Since 2016, India has held the title of the world’s largest army, surpassing China in active-duty personnel.

title: Orange production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Brazil is the world’s largest orange producer. Its production has steadily increased over the decades, reaching approximately 17.6 million tonnes in 2023, far ahead of other major producers like China and Mexico and India. 
Most of Brazil’s oranges are grown in the states of São Paulo and Minas Gerais.

title: Share of the population that is female
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, females made up approximately 28.7% of Qatar’s population, the lowest share in the world. 
This imbalance is largely due to the large number of male expatriate workers in the country.

title: Private health spending per person
infoDescription:
In 2022, the United States had the second-highest private health expenditure per person globally, at approximately $5,573 per year. 
Only Switzerland had a higher private health expenditure at almost $7,000 per person per year. 
This figure is significantly higher than that of other major countries, such as Canada ($2,012) or the United Kingdom ($1,091).

title: Self-reported life satisfaction
subtitle: Out of 10
source: Wellbeing Research Centre (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, Afghanistan had the lowest happiness score globally, with a score of 1.36 out of 10. 
This score reflects significant challenges, including ongoing conflict, political instability, and humanitarian crises. 
In contrast, Finland, the highest-ranking country, had a score of 7.74

title: Armed forces personnel as a share of total population
infoDescription:
Israel maintains a high share of armed forces personnel relative to its labor force, with about 2% serving in the military in 2021, but down from up to 4.4% in 1989.
This is significantly above the global average of around 0.3%, though lower than Eritrea’s 6.1% and North Korea’s 5.6% in the same year.

title: Share of arable land which is organic
infoDescription:
In 2022, about 27% of Austria's arable land was farmed organically.
In contrast, countries such as the United States, China, and Brazil have very low shares of organic farmland, each at around 0.5%

title: Share of people practicing open defecation
infoDescription:
Cambodia used to have extremely high levels of open defecation - 87.5% in 2000. 
Over recent decades, it has made dramatic progress, and by 2022 the share fell to around 12% of the population.

title: Soybean production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Argentina is the third-largest soybean producer in the world, behind Brazil and the United States. 
Its production saw a sharp drop in 2023, mainly due to a severe drought caused by the La Niña weather pattern. 
The extreme dryness hit the country’s main growing regions, drastically reducing yields and leading to one of its lowest harvests in over a decade.

title: Estimated nuclear warhead stockpiles
source: Federation of American Scientists (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
As of 2024, the United States is estimated to possess approximately 3,700 nuclear warheads. 
This is significantly lower than the Cold War peak of over 31,000 warheads in the 1960s.
It remains however the second-largest stockpile globally, following Russia.

title: Wealth share of the richest 1%
source: World Inequality Database (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2023, the richest 1 % in South Africa held around 55% of the country’s wealth - the highest share in the world. 
In comparison, the top 1 % in the United States held about 35%, and in the United Kingdom about 21%.
The lowest share in the world is 13.9% in the Netherlands.

title: Sugar cane production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Brazil is the world’s top sugar‑cane producer, with recent production reaching almost 600 million tonnes per year. 
Its output far surpasses that of other major producers like India, China, Thailand and Pakistan.

title: Suicide rate
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, Lesotho had the highest suicide rate in the world, at about 30 per 100,000 people. 
This is far lower than Russia’s peak in 2000 but makes Lesotho the country most affected by suicide in recent years.

title: Suicide rate
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
infoDescription:
In 2021, South Korea had a suicide rate of approximately 27.5 per 100,000 people, nearly doubling from 15 per 100,000 in 2000.
This sharp rise highlights a serious public health challenge, and South Korea ranks second in the world, only behind Lesotho.

title: Sunflower seed production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription: In 2023, Argentina was the world’s third-largest sunflower seed producer behind Russia and Ukraine. It used to be the world's largest producer in the 1990s but has been overtaken in the 2000s.

title: Unemployment rate
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, South Africa had an unemployment rate of approximately 33%, making it the country with the second-highest unemployment rate globally, just behind Eswatini. 
In contrast, the world average is about 5%, and the lowest unemployment rate in 2024 was Qatar with 0.1%.

title: Nuclear power generation
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
infoDescription:
As of 2024, Germany produced 0 TWh of nuclear electricity for the first time. 
Production had been decreasing since the 2011 Fukushima accident as the country gradually phased out nuclear power.

title: Unemployment rate
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, Qatar had an unemployment rate of approximately 0.1%, making it the country with the lowest unemployment rate globally.
In contrast, the world average is about 5%, and the highest unemployment rate in 2024 was Eswatini with 34.4%.

title: Deaths from homicide
subtitle: Per 100,000 people
source: United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, Jamaica had the highest homicide rate in the world, with about 49.3 per 100,000 people. 
This is just above Ecuador at 45.7 and South Africa at 43.7 per 100,000 people per year.

title: Share of electricity production from coal
infoDescription:
In 2024, coal accounted for about 74.5% of India’s electricity generation, far exceeding the global average of 34%. 
This places India among the countries most dependent on coal for electricity, with only South Africa, Botswana, Kosovo, and Mongolia reporting higher shares. 
The country’s heavy reliance on coal underscores ongoing challenges in diversifying its energy mix and reducing carbon emissions.

title: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
infoDescription:
As of 2024, the United States has launched the most objects into space, with almost 12,000 cumulative launches, largely due to recent SpaceX’s Starlink satellites. 
In comparison, Russia has launched 3,925 objects and China 1,385, highlighting U.S. dominance in space activity.

title: Nuclear power generation
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
infoDescription:
As of 2024, the United States remains the world leader in nuclear energy generation, producing approximately 782 TWh, which accounts for about 28% of global nuclear electricity generation. 
China is second with 445 TWh, while France is third with 379 TWh in 2024.

title: Nuclear power generation
subtitle: In terawatt-hours
infoDescription:
As of 2024, France was the third-largest producer of nuclear electricity in the world, after the United States and China. 
Other major producers include Russia and South Korea.

title: Number of women who die from maternal conditions
infoDescription:
In India, maternal deaths (death of women during or shortly after pregnancy) fell dramatically from about 180,000 deaths in 1985 to less than 20,000 in 2023. 
This sharp decline reflects major improvements in maternal healthcare and safe childbirth practices.
Nigeria is now the country in the world with the highest number of maternal deaths per year.

title: Soybean production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
The United States was the largest soybean producer in the world for decades until it was overtaken by Brazil in 2019. 
Despite this, it remains the second-largest producer, with output staying close to Brazil’s in recent years. 
Argentina and China rank third and fourth, but their production levels are considerably lower.

title: Share of electricity production from solar
infoDescription:
In 2024, solar power generated 22.3% of Chile’s electricity, one of the highest shares in the world. 
It experienced a rapid and sustained expansion from 0% in 2012. 
By 2023, Chile had surpassed countries like Spain, Italy, and Australia, becoming a global leader in solar electricity generation.

title: Methane emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
The United States was the world’s third-largest methane emitter, behind China and India in 2022.
Its emissions mainly came from the energy sector - especially oil and gas extraction - along with agriculture and waste, placing it behind China and India.

title: Methane emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Russia was once the world’s largest methane emitter, but its emissions fell sharply in the 1990s.
The drop came mainly from the post-Soviet economic collapse, which reduced coal production, gas output, and livestock numbers. 
Today Russia emits far less than China, India, and the United States, which have all surpassed it.

title: Methane emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Venezuela used to be one of the world’s higher methane emitters, but its emissions have fallen sharply since around 2015.
A major reason is the contraction of its oil and gas industry, driven by economic collapse and strengthened U.S. sanctions that severely reduced production and exports.
As a result, methane leakage from wells and pipelines declined, leaving Venezuela far below major emitters such as China, India, and the United States.

title: Per capita methane emissions
subtitle: In kg
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Qatar has the highest per-capita methane emissions in the world, largely because of its intensive natural gas extraction and processing sector, which releases significant methane relative to its small population. 
It is followed by Bahrain and Equatorial Guinea, which rank second and third globally but remain well below Qatar’s levels.

title: Per capita methane emissions
subtitle: In kg
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Bahrain has the world’s second-highest per-capita methane emissions, driven mainly by its oil and gas industry, where methane leakage from extraction and refining contributes heavily to the total. 
Only Qatar has a higher per capita methane emission.

title: Almond production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
The United States is by far the largest producer of almonds globally, supplying roughly half of the world’s almonds, about 1.8 million tonnes in 2023. 
California’s favorable climate and high-yield almond farming make it the backbone of this dominance. 
As a result, U.S. almonds play an outsized role in global supply, exports, and the global nuts market.

title: Almond production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Italy used to be the world’s top almond producer in the 1960s, but today its output is much more modest. 
As of 2023, the top three producers are the United States, Spain, and Australia.

title: Almond production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Spain is the world’s second‑largest almond producer, trailing only the United States. 
Its annual output has recently recovered to about 300,000 tonnes, reinforcing its position in global almond supply. 
Much of this production comes from regions like Andalusia, Aragon, and Castilla‑La Mancha, where almonds make up the majority of the country’s nut cultivation.

title: Artichoke production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Egypt overtook Italy in 2021 to become the world’s largest producer of artichokes. 
Its rise reflects expanding cultivation and favorable growing conditions, making Egypt the top global supplier today.

title: Avocado production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Mexico is the world’s largest producer of avocados, supplying roughly 28% of global avocado output. 
The following biggest producers are Colombia, the Dominican Republic, Peru and Indonesia.

title: Cherry production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Turkey is the world’s largest cherry producer, with an output of over 700,000 tonnes per year. 
It leads global production ahead of countries like Chile and the United States, producing roughly a quarter of the world’s cherries.

title: Coconut production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription: Indonesia is the world’s largest producer of coconuts, just ahead of the Philippines and India, producing over 18 million tonnes annually and supplying a significant share of the global coconut market.

title: Coconut production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
India is the world’s third-largest coconut producer, following Indonesia and the Philippines. 
It produces roughly 14 million tonnes annually, contributing significantly to global coconut supply.

title: Date production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Saudi Arabia is the world’s second-largest date producer, following Egypt and ahead of Algeria. 
It produces around 1.6 million tonnes annually, making it a key supplier in the global date market.

title: Date production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Algeria is the world’s third-largest date producer, behind Egypt and Saudi Arabia. 
Its annual production is roughly 1.3 million tonnes, contributing significantly to global supply.

title: Exports of eggs
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
The Netherlands is the world’s largest exporter of eggs, thanks to its highly efficient poultry industry and strong production capacity. 
A large portion of these exports goes to neighboring Germany, which is the world's biggest importer of eggs.

title: Imports of eggs
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Germany is the world’s largest importer of eggs, with a large portion of its supply coming from neighboring Netherlands, the world’s biggest egg exporter. 
The Netherlands’ highly efficient poultry industry and strong production capacity support this dominant export position.

title: Exports of lemons and limes
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Mexico is the world’s second-largest exporter of lemons and limes, after Argentina and ahead of South Africa. 
Most of its exports go to the United States, with additional shipments to European countries, making it a key player in the global market.

title: Exports of lemons and limes
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
South Africa is the world’s third‑largest exporter of lemons and limes behind Argentina and Mexico. 
Its exports have risen sharply in recent years, making South Africa a rapidly emerging player in the global citrus market.

title: Corn production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Brazil ranks third worldwide behind the United States and China, with production around 130 million tonnes in 2023. 
Its corn output has been rising rapidly, helped by favorable growing seasons and the ability to harvest two crops per year in some regions, making Brazil a growing competitor in global corn supply.

title: Exports of milk
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
The Netherlands is the world’s largest exporter of milk, thanks to its highly efficient dairy sector and strong focus on international markets. 
Its strategic location and advanced processing industry make it the leading supplier globally ahead of Germany and New Zealand.

title: Exports of milk
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Germany is the second-largest milk exporter in the world behind the Netherlands, supported by a massive dairy industry and strong demand from nearby European markets. 
Its well-developed infrastructure helps sustain consistently high export volumes.

title: Olive oil production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Spain is the world’s largest producer of olive oil, far ahead of all competitors, with output levels often more than double those of the next-largest producers such as Italy and Greece.
2022 was a bad season mainly caused by extreme drought and record-high summer temperatures across Andalusia, the country’s key olive-growing region.

title: Olive oil imports
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Although Italy is the world’s second-largest olive oil producer, its domestic output does not meet the country’s high internal demand - driven by both consumers and its large food-processing industry. 
As a result, Italy is the world’s largest importer of olive oil, bringing in substantial volumes each year, mainly from Spain, Greece, and Tunisia to supplement its own production.

title: Palm oil production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription: Indonesia is by far the world’s largest palm-oil producer, supplying roughly 60 % of global output - more than twice that of Malaysia, the second-largest producer.

title: Pepper production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Brazil ranks second globally in pepper production, contributing around 15% of the world’s total. 
While far behind Vietnam, Brazil’s output is more than double that of many other producers, making it a major alternative source for pepper.

title: Pineapple production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Indonesia is the world’s leading producer of pineapples, with around 3.16 million tonnes in 2023. 
It tops global output, ahead of the Philippines and Costa Rica.

title: Pineapple production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription: The Philippines ranks second in global pineapple production, with roughly 2.94 million tonnes in 2023, just behind Indonesia and slightly ahead of Costa Rica.

title: Raspberry production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Russia is the world’s largest raspberry producer, with about 219,000 tonnes in 2023. 
Most raspberries are grown in the temperate regions of southern Russia and around the North Caucasus, where the climate and soil are ideal for berry cultivation.
Behind Russia, Mexico, Serbia and Poland are also major global producers.

title: Rye production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Russia was once a global rye powerhouse, producing around 13.9 million tonnes in 1992. 
Since then, production has plummeted to roughly 1.4 million tonnes in recent years, due to reduced land area for rye, shifts toward more profitable grains like wheat, and declining domestic demand. 
Germany and Poland have since surpassed Russia, becoming the world’s leading rye producers.

title: Rye production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Poland ranks second globally, producing roughly 2.53 million tonnes in 2023. 
Rye is a traditional staple in Polish agriculture, especially in the northern and central regions. 
Much of the harvest is used domestically for bread and spirits, though some is exported to nearby European markets.

title: Exports of tomatoes
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Italy is the world’s largest exporter of tomatoes, supplying more tomatoes to the global market than China, Spain and Turkey.
Its strong production infrastructure and established processing and export systems help maintain its leading export position.

title: Imports of tomatoes
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Germany is the world’s largest importers of tomatoes, ahead of the United Kingdom.
A large share of its imported tomatoes come from nearby suppliers such as the Netherlands, Spain, and Morocco.
Note: the FAO applied a methodological change from the year 2010 onwards.

title: Nitrogen oxides emissions
subtitle: In million tonnes
source: Hoesly et al. (2024) (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
China’s NOₓ emissions rose sharply during its rapid industrialization and economic boom, reaching a peak in 2012. 
In recent years, however, emissions have begun to decline as a result of efforts to reduce coal dependence, tighten emissions standards, and shift toward cleaner energy.

title: Copper production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Peru is the world’s second-largest copper producer, contributing roughly 12% of global production in 2023. 
Its strong growth over the past two decades has firmly positioned it behind Chile and just ahead of the DRC.

title: Copper production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
The DRC is now the third-largest copper producer globally, with around 11% of world output in 2023. 
Rapid expansion of its mining sector has allowed it to overtake several competitors and close the gap with Peru, but it is stiff far behind Chile, the top-producer.

title: Maternal mortality ratio
subtitle: Number of women who die from maternal conditions per 100,000 live births
source: UN MMEIG (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
South Sudan has the highest maternal mortality ratio in the world, with more than 1,200 deaths per 100,000 live births. 
This extremely high rate is driven by limited access to skilled birth attendants, very low availability of functioning health facilities, widespread home births, and severe shortages in emergency obstetric care. 
Decades of conflict and displacement have further weakened the health system, making childbirth one of the most dangerous experiences for women in the country.
Chad and Nigeria are the two only other countries in the world with a meternal mortality ratio of more than 1,000 as of 2020.

title: Maternal mortality ratio
subtitle: Number of women who die from maternal conditions per 100,000 live births
source: UN MMEIG (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Sierra Leone has achieved one of the most dramatic improvements in maternal mortality anywhere in the world. 
In 1999, its maternal mortality ratio was about 1,792 deaths per 100,000 live births, an extremely high level. By 2020, this had fallen to roughly 440 deaths per 100,000, a reduction of around 75%.
This remarkable progress is linked to better access to skilled birth attendants, expanded emergency obstetric care, and nationwide policies that made maternal healthcare more accessible and affordable.

title: Aluminum production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
China is by far the world’s largest aluminum producer, accounting for nearly 60 % of global output. 
This dominant share reflects massive smelting capacity and the country’s role as the central hub for aluminum processing worldwide. 
China’s output dwarfs that of any other country, with India and Russia being second and third largest producers.

title: Bauxite production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
Guinea is the second-largest bauxite producer behinf Australia and ahead of China, contributing roughly 24% of global production. 
Its rapidly expanding industry has brought it close to Australia, though it remains slightly behind. 
Bauxite is used mainly for aluminum production, feeding global demand for lightweight metals used in infrastructure, vehicles, and manufacturing.

title: Share of the population that is undernourished
infoDescription:
In 2022, an estimated 50.4% of Haiti's population was undernourished.
Only in Somalia is the rate higher, at 51.3%. But while Somalia's rate decresed in the past decade, Haiti rose from a low of 38% in 2014.

title: Gold production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
China is the world’s largest gold producer, producing about 375 tonnes of gold in 2023, or roughly 12% of global mine output.
Its production has been rising consistently until it peaked in 2016.
Behind China, the next largest gold producers are Russia in second place and Australia in third.

title: Gold production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Australia is the third-largest gold producer in the world, mining almost 300 tonnes of gold in 2023, equal to roughly 9% of global production.
Its output had a sharp increase in the 1980s, driven by large, long-life mines in Western Australia.
Only China and Russia produced more gold than Australia in 2023.

title: Gold production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Russia is the second-largest gold producer in the world, mining roughly 313 tonnes in 2023. 
Output has increased steadily since the 2000s due to large-scale mining in Siberia and the Far East, with only China producing more gold and Australia ranking just behind Russia.

title: Silver production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
China is the world’s second-largest silver producer, contributing roughly 13% of global mine output in 2023. 
It ranks behind Mexico, which clearly dominates global production, and slightly ahead of Peru.

title: Silver production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Peru is the world’s third-largest silver producer, accounting for around 12% of global mine output in 2023. 
It trails Mexico and China but remains a major player in global silver supply, with production well ahead of other countries such as Australia and the United States.

title: Uranium production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Kazakhstan is the world’s top uranium producer by a wide margin, supplying roughly 40% of global mined uranium in 2023. 
Its dominance stems from large, low-cost in-situ recovery operations that have expanded rapidly since the 2000s, overtaking Canada and Australia to lead global output.

title: Uranium production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Namibia is the world’s third-largest uranium producer as of 2023. 
It ranks behind Kazakhstan and Canada, but ahead of Australia and Uzbekistan, making Namibia a key pillar of global uranium supply despite its much smaller population and economy.

title: Uranium production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Australia is the world’s fourth-largest uranium producer in 2023, behind Kazakhstan, Canada, and Namibia. 
Its share of global output has declined over time, from accounting to about 22% of the global productions in the 2000s, to less than 10% in 2023.

title: Uranium production as a share of the global total
infoDescription:
In 1970, the Unites States accounted for about 54% of the world’s uranium output, making it by far the dominant producer at the time. 
By 2023, that share had fallen to just 0.14%, reflecting the sharp decline of domestic mining activity as U.S. producers slowed operations and production shifted to lower-cost regions like Kazakhstan, Canada, and Namibia.

title: Known crude oil reserves
subtitle: In billion cubic meters
source: US Energy Information Administration (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Saudi Arabia long held the world’s largest known crude oil reserves, until it was overtaken by Venezuela in 2013. 
Venezuela’s jump came from a major upward revision of its reserves, while Saudi Arabia’s reserves remained broadly stable since the 1990s. 
Today, Saudi Arabia ranks second globally, ahead of Iran in third place.

title: Known crude oil reserves
subtitle: In billion cubic meters
source: US Energy Information Administration (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Iran holds the third-largest known crude oil reserves in the world, with around 200 billion barrels. 
Its reported reserves saw a notable upward revision around 2021, reflecting both reassessments of existing fields and broader global trends in reserve reporting. 
Venezuela and Saudi Arabia remain first and second, respectively, with Iran trailing closely behind in the global ranking.

title: Known crude oil reserves
subtitle: In billion cubic meters
source: US Energy Information Administration (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Canada has the world’s fourth-largest known crude oil reserves, at roughly 160 billion barrels.
The sharp jump around 2003 reflects the inclusion of Alberta’s oil sands as economically recoverable reserves, which now account for most of Canada’s total.

title: Beer consumption per person
subtitle: In liters
infoDescription:
Germany has one of the world’s highest beer consumption rates per person, but its per-capita consumption has declined significantly over time. 
At its peak in 1976, Germans drank about 190 litres of beer per person per year; by 2020 this had fallen to around 100 litres, reflecting changing drinking habits and broader shifts in lifestyle and health preferences.

title: Cashew nut production
subtitle: In million tonnes
infoDescription:
India is the world’s second-largest producer of cashew nuts, ranking behind Ivory Coast and ahead of Vietnam.
Indian cashew production has grown over recent decades, supported by cultivation in states like Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and Karnataka, and serves both domestic consumption and export markets.

title: Share of women in ministerial positions
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
As of 2024, Finland has the second-highest share of women in ministerial positions in the world, with women holding around 61% of all cabinet posts. 
This puts Finland only behind Nicaragua, making it a global outlier in female representation at the ministerial level.

title: Pistachio production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
The United States is the world’s top producer of pistachios. 
Iran ranks second, followed by China in third place, both producing substantially less than the U.S. 
Within the United States, pistachios are grown almost entirely in California, especially in the Central Valley, where the hot, dry summers and cool winters provide ideal conditions for pistachio orchards.

title: Pistachio production
subtitle: In tonnes
infoDescription:
Turkey is the world’s third-largest producer of pistachios, ranking behind the United States and Iran. 
While its total production is lower than that of the top two producers, Turkey remains a significant contributor to global pistachio supply and plays an important role in regional markets.

title: Average years of schooling
infoDescription:
Yemen’s mean years of schooling rose steeply from approximately 0.3 years in 1990 to about 5.5 years by 2023, representing the largest percentage increase in the world.
Despite starting from the lowest bases globally, Yemen’s rate of growth substantially outpaced many other nations, but is still below the global average of about 8.8 years of schooling.

title: Gross domestic product (GDP)
subtitle: In US dollars adjusted for inflation
source: Eurostat, OECD, IMF, and World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
The United States is the world’s second-largest economy as of 2024, with GDP of about $25.7 trillion, only behind China. 
It was overtaken in 2016 by China, when the US produced roughly $21.1 trillion compared with China’s $21.9 trillion, marking a shift in global economic leadership.

title: Gross domestic product (GDP)
subtitle: In US dollars adjusted for inflation
source: Eurostat, OECD, IMF, and World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
India is the world’s third-largest economy as of 2024, with GDP of roughly $14 trillion, reflecting its rapid growth and expanding global role. 
It now sits behind China and the United States, cementing India’s position among the leading economic powers.

title: Gross domestic product (GDP)
subtitle: In US dollars adjusted for inflation
source: Eurostat, OECD, IMF, and World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
Russia ranks as the world’s fourth-largest economy as of 2024, with GDP of roughly $6.9 trillion, behind China, the United States and India. 
It sits just ahead of Japan and Germany, highlighting Russia’s continued economic scale despite close competition among the next tier of major economies.

title: Inflation of consumer prices
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2024, Turkey had an inflation rate of approximately 58%, making it the highest in the world, ahead of Lebanon which had a 221% inflation rate in 2023.
This rate reflects ongoing economic pressures, including currency depreciation and rising costs, which have significantly affected the cost of living.

title: Inflation of consumer prices
source: World Bank (via Our World in Data)
infoDescription:
In 2023, Lebanon experienced an inflation rate of approximately 221%. 
This surge was primarily driven by the steep depreciation of the Lebanese pound during the first half of the year. 
It decreased to 45% in 2024, with only Turkey having a higher inflation rate.
